q: RA 8050 is commonly known as the:
w: RA 8050 is the Revised Optometry Law of 1995; it repealed the older RA 1998.
k: RA 8050 = Revised Optometry Law of 1995.

q: RA 8050 repealed which earlier law?
w: Despite the confusing numbers, RA 8050 (1995) is the current law and it repealed RA 1998.
k: RA 8050 repealed RA 1998.

q: The Board of Optometry is composed of how many members?
w: Five members, appointed by the President from 10 nominees of the accredited national organization.
k: Board of Optometry = 5 members.

q: Board members are appointed from a list of how many nominees?
w: The accredited national organization submits 10 nominees for the 5 Board seats.
k: 5 members from 10 nominees.

q: The term of office of a Board of Optometry member is:
w: Members serve a 5-year non-renewable term, staggered so one rotates out yearly.
k: Board term = 5 years, non-renewable.

q: How many Board members constitute a quorum and are needed to decide an administrative case?
w: Three members form a quorum and three votes are required for an administrative decision.
k: Quorum / admin vote = 3.

q: To pass the licensure examination, a candidate must obtain:
w: Section 20: at least a 75% general average AND no subject below 50%.
k: Pass = 75% avg, none below 50%.

q: A candidate who fails the board exam how many times must take a refresher course?
w: After failing three times, a refresher course is required before re-examination.
k: Fail 3x → refresher course.

q: The professional license of an optometrist is renewed every:
w: Licenses renew every 3 years, requiring continuing education and fees.
k: License renewal every 3 years.

q: A revocation or suspension may be appealed to the PRC within:
w: Section 27: appeal within 15 days of receiving the Board's decision.
k: Appeal window = 15 days.

q: The penalty for violating RA 8050 (prohibited acts) is:
o: A fine only, up to P5,000
w: Section 33: 1 to 8 years imprisonment, or P10,000 to P40,000 fine, or both, plus administrative penalties.
k: Penalty: 1–8 years, P10,000–40,000, or both.

q: Under RA 8050, a public health worker in the blindness-prevention program may perform:
w: Section 5 limits trained public health workers to visual acuity and screening only.
k: Public health workers: VA + screening only.

q: Which agency supervises the Board of Optometry?
w: The Board operates under the general supervision of the PRC.
k: Board is under the PRC.

q: An optometrist who advertises that they charge 'lower fees than any other clinic' is:
o: Allowed if the claim is true
w: Section 32(c) prohibits comparative/price advertising; truth is no defense.
k: Comparative/price advertising = prohibited act.

q: To be eligible for the licensure exam, an applicant must hold a Doctor of Optometry degree after a:
w: Section 19 requires a Filipino citizen of good moral character with a 6-year O.D. degree.
k: 6-year O.D. course required.

q: Who may legally examine the human eye WITHOUT an optometry license under RA 8050?
w: Section 5 exempts physicians with post-graduate training in eye disease; their physical-exam eye check is not 'practicing optometry'.
k: Physician with eye training is exempt.

q: Before an optometrist trained before the law may use DPAs, Section 31 requires:
w: Those admitted before the law must pass a special pharmacology certification before using DPAs.
k: DPA use needs pharmacology certification (Sec 31).

q: Board members are appointed by:
w: The President appoints the 5 members on PRC recommendation from the organization's 10 nominees.
k: Board appointed by the President.

q: The licensure examination is given at least:
w: Section 17: the Board gives the examination at least once every year.
k: Licensure exam at least once a year.

q: The Board reports examination ratings to the PRC within:
w: Section 21: ratings are reported within 60 days after the last examination day.
k: Results to PRC within 60 days.

q: How many duties does the Code of Professional Ethics (Sec 40) require of an optometrist?
w: Section 40 lists six duties: visual welfare of the public, best standards, continuous proficiency, equal diligence, confidentiality, and referral.
k: Sec 40 = 6 ethical duties.

q: All optometrists are integrated into:
w: RA 8050 integrates optometrists into one national organization that promulgates the Code of Ethics.
k: Integration into one national organization.

q: An optometrist must, on documents, indicate the license number and expiry, and in the clinic must:
w: Section 6 (disclosure) requires displaying the COR conspicuously and stating the license number/expiry on documents.
k: Display the COR; state license number.

q: A Board member may be removed for neglect, incompetence, or malpractice by:
w: Section 10: the President may remove a member for cause after due process by the PRC.
k: Board member removed by the President (for cause).

q: The State policy in RA 8050 declares optometry to be:
w: Section 2 recognizes optometry as essential and part of primary health care.
k: Optometry = part of primary health care.

q: Engaging in optometry without a license (unauthorized practice) is addressed in:
w: Unauthorized practice is prohibited under Section 5 and is a punishable prohibited act under Section 32.
k: Unauthorized practice = prohibited (Sec 5/32).

q: How many DPAs are authorized under RA 8050?
w: Five approved DPAs: phenylephrine, tropicamide, cyclopentolate, proparacaine, fluorescein (plus authorized miotics).
k: 5 approved DPAs.

q: After passing and before registration, the successful examinee must:
o: Take an oath before the Board
w: Section 23: successful examinees take their oath before the certificate of registration is issued.
k: Oath taken before registration.

q: A requirement to sit the licensure exam is that the applicant be a:
w: Section 19 requires Filipino citizenship, good moral character, and the 6-year O.D. degree.
k: Applicant must be a Filipino citizen.

q: Which best states the relationship between latent and total hyperopia in the law's scope? (Trick: which is a LAW fact, not a clinical one?)
o: RA 8050 sets the Board at 5 members
w: The Board of Optometry has five members, appointed by the President from ten nominees. The other choices are clinical facts from other subjects, not jurisprudence.
k: Board of Optometry = 5 members from 10 nominees.

q: Comparative advertising and offering free examinations are:
w: Both comparative/price advertising and free-service inducements are prohibited (Sec 32 and Board resolutions).
k: Free-service and price ads are prohibited.

q: A violation of the Code of Professional Ethics is treated as:
w: Section 40 states a Code violation is unethical conduct, punishable by suspension/prohibition under Section 26.
k: Code violation → suspension (Sec 26).

q: The four core bioethical principles are:
w: Beauchamp and Childress: autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice.
k: Four principles: autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice.

q: Informed consent is the direct expression of which principle?
w: Respecting the competent patient's right to decide (informed consent) is autonomy.
k: Informed consent = autonomy.

q: 'Primum non nocere' (first, do no harm) is the principle of:
w: Non-maleficence is the obligation to avoid harm; benefit must outweigh harm.
k: Primum non nocere = non-maleficence.

q: Treating a charity (PhilHealth) patient with a quicker, lesser exam than a private patient violates:
w: Justice requires the same standard of care regardless of ability to pay or status.
k: Unequal care = justice violation.

q: Instilling a DPA without explaining the effects or obtaining permission violates:
w: Failing to disclose and obtain consent denies the patient's right to decide (autonomy).
k: No consent for DPA = autonomy violation.

q: Recommending an unnecessary spectacle upgrade for commercial gain violates:
w: Acting against the patient's best interest for profit violates beneficence (doing good).
k: Unneeded upgrade for profit = beneficence violation.

q: Informed consent requires that the patient understands all EXCEPT:
w: Consent requires disclosure of diagnosis, options, risks, and alternatives, with voluntary agreement; not the clinician's income.
k: Consent = diagnosis, options, risks, alternatives.

q: For a minor patient, consent is provided by:
w: A parent or guardian consents; assent is sought from older children.
k: Minor: guardian consents, child assents.

q: In a true emergency where delay would cause serious harm, informed consent:
o: May be ignored in all cases
May be waived under the doctrine
Must be obtained from the PRC
w: Consent may be waived in emergencies when delay would cause serious harm.
k: Emergency: consent may be waived.

q: A competent adult refuses your referral for a suspicious optic disc. The most ethical action is to:
w: Autonomy allows refusal, but beneficence/non-maleficence require continued care, not abandonment.
k: Refusal: document, re-counsel, do not abandon.

q: Declining not to fit risky extended-wear lenses in a non-compliant patient with prior keratitis reflects:
w: Avoiding a foreseeable harm whose risk outweighs benefit is non-maleficence.
k: Avoiding foreseeable harm = non-maleficence.

q: Non-maleficence means:
w: It is a balance, not a ban; doing nothing is also a choice with consequences.
k: Non-maleficence = benefit must beat harm.

q: The four-principle framework is credited to:
w: Beauchamp and Childress described the four-principle framework of biomedical ethics.
k: Four principles = Beauchamp & Childress.

q: Keeping patient records and diagnoses private is the duty of:
w: Confidentiality protects privileged patient information; it is a specific application of trust/autonomy.
k: Privacy of records = confidentiality.

q: Referring a condition that exceeds your competence reflects which principle?
w: Referring when a condition is beyond your scope prevents harm (non-maleficence).
k: Refer beyond scope = non-maleficence.

q: A company offers a gift to prescribe their lenses exclusively. Declining is supported mainly by:
w: Lens selection must follow patient need (beneficence) and fairness (justice), not kickbacks.
k: Reject kickbacks: beneficence + justice.

q: Informed consent is best described as:
o: A legal loophole for the clinic
w: Consent requires genuine understanding, not just a signature; it is the expression of autonomy.
k: Consent = understanding, not just a signature.

q: The Section 40 duty 'render services with equal diligence to all regardless of race, creed or status' maps to:
w: Equal diligence to all is the principle of justice.
k: Equal diligence to all = justice.

q: The Section 40 duty 'consider the visual welfare of the public as the primary responsibility' maps to:
w: Acting in the patient's/public's best interest is beneficence.
k: Visual welfare of public = beneficence.

q: A minor arrives alone requesting a contact lens fitting. The correct action is to:
w: Autonomy/consent rules require parent or guardian consent for a minor; defer the fitting.
k: Minor alone → defer for guardian consent.

q: The legal doctrine 'let the master answer', making a superior responsible for a subordinate's actions, is:
w: Respondeat superior holds the supervising professional responsible for delegated acts.
k: Respondeat superior = 'let the master answer'.

q: The obligation to keep current through continuing education is the principle of:
w: Continuous competence (Sec 40: 'continuously enhance proficiency') ties to the 3-year CPD renewal.
k: CPD / staying current = continuous competence.

q: Law and ethics differ in that:
w: Law tells you what you must do under penalty; ethics tells you what you ought to do (though RA 8050 makes Code violations punishable).
k: Law = enforceable rules; ethics = moral obligation.

q: Which principle is most commonly tested through scenarios about contraindications and referral?
w: Non-maleficence (avoid harm, refer, respect contraindications) and autonomy (consent) are the most tested.
k: Contraindication/referral scenarios = non-maleficence.

q: The correct order to register a sole-proprietorship optometry clinic is:
w: Register the name (DTI), clear the location (Barangay), get the permit (Mayor), then register for taxes (BIR).
k: Setup: DTI → Barangay → Mayor → BIR.

q: The DTI registration provides the:
w: DTI registers the business name; the certificate is valid for 5 years.
k: DTI = business name (5-year cert).

q: Registration with the BIR provides the:
w: The BIR issues the certificate of registration, TIN, and registers the books of accounts for taxes.
k: BIR = TIN + books of accounts.

q: Under Board Resolution No. 11 (2016), an optometrist must notify the Board of the clinic location within:
w: The clinic location (with photographs) must be reported to the Board within 30 days of securing the premises.
k: Notify Board of clinic location in 30 days.

q: The minimum examination room area required is:
w: The exam/treatment room must be at least 10 square meters with a test distance of at least 3 meters.
k: Exam room ≥ 10 sq m.

q: The minimum test distance for the examination room is:
w: At least a 3-meter test distance is required (the room must be ≥10 sq m).
k: Test distance ≥ 3 m.

q: The amount of chair time and the choice of method during an exam is:
w: Board Resolution No. 11 makes chair time and method the optometrist's sole discretion, free of external influence.
k: Chair time = optometrist's sole discretion.

q: Permissible optometric advertising must be:
w: Only true, honest, dignified, objective information is allowed; price and superiority claims are prohibited.
k: Advertising: true, honest, dignified, objective.

q: Which advertisement is PROHIBITED?
o: A dignified statement of services offered
w: Advertising free or discounted services (and price/superiority claims) is prohibited.
k: Free-service / price ads are prohibited.

q: An optometry clinic must display all of the following EXCEPT:
w: Display CORs, PICs, Certificates of Good Standing, and CPD certificates; only optometry-related documents.
k: Display COR, PIC, COGS, CPD (only optometry docs).

q: The product (frames/sunglasses) display area must:
o: Be the main feature identifying the practice
NOT be used to highlight or identify the practice
w: Products must not be used to identify the practice, to protect optometry's professional image.
k: Product display ≠ identify the practice.

q: Mandatory employee government contributions in a Philippine clinic include:
w: Employers must remit SSS, PhilHealth, and Pag-IBIG contributions for staff.
k: Employee benefits: SSS, PhilHealth, Pag-IBIG.

q: The Occupational Safety and Health Standards (OSHS) are administered by:
w: OSHS falls under the Department of Labor and Employment (DOLE).
k: OSHS under DOLE.

q: Maternity leave under RA 11210 is:
w: The Expanded Maternity Leave Law (RA 11210) grants 105 days.
k: Maternity leave = 105 days (RA 11210).

q: Paternity leave under RA 8187 is:
w: RA 8187 grants 7 working days of paternity leave.
k: Paternity leave = 7 days (RA 8187).

q: GSIS covers which workers, and SSS which?
w: GSIS covers government employees; SSS covers the private sector.
k: GSIS = government; SSS = private.

q: During operating hours, the optometry clinic must be:
w: Board Resolution No. 11 requires a licensed optometrist on-site during regular operating hours.
k: Clinic manned by a licensed optometrist.

q: Required instruments for ocular examination include all EXCEPT:
w: Required instruments are clinical (slit lamp, ophthalmoscope, retinoscope, phoropter, trial set, VA chart, Ishihara, Amsler); a cash register is not clinical equipment.
k: Clinical instruments required (not retail equipment).

q: The optometry clinic space must be:
w: Board Resolution No. 11 requires an enclosed, exclusive professional setup separate from commercial concerns.
k: Clinic used exclusively for optometry.

q: A non-optometrist staff member may:
o: Perform the acts constituting the practice of optometry
Not perform acts that constitute the practice of optometry
w: Only a licensed optometrist may perform the practice of optometry; the optometrist remains responsible for delegated tasks (respondeat superior).
k: Only the optometrist may practice optometry.

q: Solicitation of patients by canvassing or employing agents/solicitors is:
w: Board resolutions prohibit soliciting patients via canvassing, agents, entertainers, or organizations.
k: Soliciting patients (canvassing) is prohibited.

q: The local business permit to operate the clinic is issued by:
w: The Mayor's office issues the business permit after inspections (fire, sanitation, electrical).
k: Mayor's office = business permit.

q: The Board of Optometry is composed of how many members, chosen from how many nominees?
w: Five members are appointed by the President from ten nominees submitted by the accredited professional organization through the PRC.
k: Board = 5 members from 10 nominees.

q: The term of a Board of Optometry member is:
w: Members serve a single non-renewable five-year term, which protects Board independence.
k: Board term = 5 years, non-renewable.

q: An optometrist's professional license must be renewed every:
w: Renewal runs on a three-year cycle with the CPD units required by the PRC.
k: License renewal every 3 years.

q: A candidate who fails the licensure examination three times must:
w: After three failures the candidate must complete a refresher course before being readmitted to the examination.
k: Fail 3× = refresher course.

q: The window to appeal a Board decision is:
w: An appeal must be filed within 15 days of the decision, first to the PRC and then to the Court of Appeals.
k: Appeal window = 15 days.

q: The Board of Optometry operates under which agency?
w: The Board sits under the PRC, which supervises all the professional regulatory boards.
k: Board of Optometry under the PRC.

q: The four principles of biomedical ethics were formulated by:
w: Beauchamp and Childress set out autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence and justice as the working framework of biomedical ethics.
k: Four principles = Beauchamp and Childress.

q: Informed consent is chiefly an expression of which principle?
w: Consent exists to protect the patient's right to decide for themselves, which is autonomy. Beneficence is acting for their good; justice is fair distribution.
k: Informed consent = autonomy.

q: 'Primum non nocere' translates as:
w: Primum non nocere is the Latin of non-maleficence: the duty to avoid inflicting harm, which includes referring beyond your competence.
k: Primum non nocere = first, do no harm.

q: Referring a patient whose condition is beyond your scope of practice expresses:
w: Practising past your competence risks harm, so referring on is the non-maleficent act.
k: Refer beyond scope = non-maleficence.

q: Treating every patient equally regardless of social or economic status is:
w: Distributive justice concerns the fair allocation of care and attention, without regard to a patient's standing or ability to pay.
k: Equal care regardless of status = justice.

q: When a minor is treated, the parent gives consent and the child gives:
w: The guardian holds legal consent, but the child's developmentally appropriate agreement, assent, should still be sought as a matter of respect.
k: Parent consents; child assents.

q: The Code of Professional Ethics for optometrists is found in:
w: Section 40 contains the Code of Ethics. Section 26 carries the penalty for violating it: suspension or prohibition from practice.
k: Code of Ethics = RA 8050 Sec 40; penalty Sec 26.

q: The correct order for setting up a practice as a business is:
w: Register the business name with DTI, clear the barangay, secure the mayor's permit, then register with BIR for the TIN and books of accounts.
k: DTI → Barangay → Mayor → BIR.

q: The DTI issues a business name certificate valid for:
w: The DTI certificate of business name registration runs five years and is then renewed.
k: DTI business name = 5-year certificate.

q: The Board must be notified of a change of clinic location within:
w: A change of practice address must reach the Board within 30 days so the register stays accurate.
k: Notify Board of location change within 30 days.

q: The minimum floor area for an optometric examination room is:
w: Board Resolution No. 11 (2016) sets a minimum of 10 square metres, with a minimum testing distance of 3 metres.
k: Exam room ≥10 sq m; test distance ≥3 m.

q: The minimum testing distance required in an examination room is:
w: Three metres is the minimum, with mirrors used to achieve the optical equivalent of 6 m where the room is short.
k: Minimum test distance = 3 m.

q: Optometric office standards are governed by:
w: Board Resolution No. 11 (2016) governs office standards. Advertising is governed by Board Resolution No. 1 (2008) together with RA 8050 Section 32.
k: Office standards = BR 11 (2016); advertising = BR 1 (2008).

q: Counting the 2008 IRR, the CURRENT number of diagnostic pharmaceutical agents an optometrist may use is:
w: The 2008 IRR added cyclopentolate to the original five, making six. If a question quotes RA 8050's original text the answer is five, so read the wording carefully: this is a deliberate board trap.
k: Current DPA list = 6; RA 8050 as written = 5.

q: The DPA list is set by the Board and approved by the DOH on the recommendation of the:
w: The Board sets the list, BFAD recommends, and the DOH approves. The PRC supervises the Board but does not approve the drug list.
k: DPA list: Board sets, BFAD recommends, DOH approves.

q: RA 8050 was approved on June 7, 1995 by President:
w: President Fidel V. Ramos approved RA 8050 on 7 June 1995, consolidating House Bill 14100 and Senate Bill 1998.
k: RA 8050 approved June 7, 1995 by Ramos.

q: The minimum age for a Board of Optometry member is:
w: A Board member must be a natural-born Filipino, at least 35 years old, in good standing, and in active practice for at least 10 years.
k: Board member: 35 years old, 10 years practice.

q: A Board of Optometry member must have been in active practice for at least:
w: Ten years of active practice is required, alongside the minimum age of 35 and good standing.
k: Board member: 10 years active practice.

q: Which person is DISQUALIFIED from sitting on the Board of Optometry?
o: An officer of an optometric association
w: An officer of any optometric association is disqualified. A faculty member, or anyone with a financial interest in an optometry school, must give that up first.
k: Association officers are disqualified from the Board.

q: A quorum of the Board of Optometry requires how many members?
w: Three members form a quorum, and three votes are needed to decide an administrative case.
k: Board quorum = 3; votes to decide = 3.

q: The President may suspend a Board member during an investigation for up to:
w: The President appoints and may remove or suspend a Board member, with suspension capped at 60 days pending investigation.
k: Board member suspension during probe = up to 60 days.

q: A Board member in the LAST year of his term automatically becomes:
w: The first appointees were staggered so one seat opens each year, and the member in his final year automatically chairs the Board.
k: Member in last year of term = chairperson.

q: Board examination ratings must be reported to the PRC within:
w: The Board reports ratings to the PRC within 60 days of the last examination day, and the PRC then publishes the passers alphabetically without ratings.
k: Ratings to PRC within 60 days.

q: The licensure examination must be given at least:
w: Section 17 requires the examination at least once a year, at places and dates the PRC sets.
k: Board exam: at least once a year.

q: To sit the licensure examination, the candidate's Doctor of Optometry degree must come from a course lasting:
w: Section 19 requires an O.D. from an accredited school after a six-year course. The Council of Deans, chaired by Claro M. Cinco, pushed the course from four years to six in 1980.
k: O.D. = 6-year course.

q: Which is a ground for DISQUALIFICATION from registration under Section 24?
w: Section 24 disqualifies those lacking Section 19 qualifications, who failed the exam, who were convicted by final judgment of a crime involving moral turpitude, who were found guilty of immoral conduct, or who are of unsound mind.
k: Moral turpitude conviction disqualifies registration.

q: The certificate of registration is signed by all Board members and the:
w: The certificate carries the signatures of the PRC Commissioner and all Board members, plus the seal. The professional license is the renewable card the PRC issues.
k: Certificate signed by PRC Commissioner + all Board members.

q: Under Section 5, an optometry student or graduate may examine patients only:
w: Section 5 exempts a student or graduate working under the direct supervision and control of a registered optometrist, along with trained physicians, public health workers doing screening only, reciprocity foreigners and known foreign experts.
k: Students may examine only under direct supervision.

q: Whether an act counts as the practice of optometry depends on:
o: The nature of the act itself
w: Section 4 is explicit that doing the act for free does not stop it being practice. Free screening by an unlicensed person is still illegal practice.
k: Practice is defined by the act, not by whether it was paid.

q: Section 30's Technical Panel on Optometric Education is convened by:
w: CHED convenes the Technical Panel under Section 12 of RA 7722, the Higher Education Act. It sets the minimum curriculum and monitors schools.
k: Technical Panel convened by CHED (RA 7722).

q: Under Section 33, if a corporation violates RA 8050, who is held liable?
o: No one, it is a juridical person
w: Section 33 pins liability on the managing officers when the violator is a company or corporation, so a lay corporation cannot hide behind its legal personality.
k: Corporate violation: managing officers are liable.

q: Which is a prohibited act under Section 32?
o: Advertising that one charges lower fees
w: Section 32 bars advertising that one optometrist is better than others or charges less, posing as registered, false evidence, untrained DPA use, and unauthorized practice.
k: Sec 32: no superiority or lower-fee advertising.

q: Rules and regulations under RA 8050 take effect how long after publication?
w: The IRR takes effect 30 days after publication, while the law itself took effect 15 days after publication. Do not swap the two.
k: IRR effective 30 days; law effective 15 days.

q: Already-registered optometrists were given how long to join the integrated national organization?
w: Section 36 integrates all optometrists into one national organization, with a three-year window for those already registered.
k: Integration window = 3 years.

q: A foreigner may sit the Philippine licensure examination only if:
o: He is over 35 years of age
He is endorsed by the DOH
He holds a Philippine O.D. degree
w: Section 34 rests on reciprocity. Separately, the Board may grant a special permit to an internationally known foreign expert.
k: Foreigners sit the exam only on reciprocity.

q: The first regulatory optometry law in the Philippines was passed in:
w: The Optometry Law of 1917, written in Spanish, was the first regulatory law and gave optometry legal recognition as a profession. The chain runs 1917 → RA 1998 (1957) → RA 8050 (1995).
k: First optometry law = 1917; chain 1917 → 1998 → 8050.

q: The 1917 Optometry Law created which examining body?
w: The 1917 law created the Junta Examinadora, with a grandfather clause nicknamed 'examinacion de gracia' for those already holding an office a year before 11 March 1917.
k: 1917 law created the Junta Examinadora.

q: Jose Rizal trained in ophthalmology in Paris under:
w: Rizal trained under Louis de Wecker in Paris in 1885, and also under Otto Becker in Germany and Ernst Fuchs in Vienna. He operated on his mother Teodora Alonzo's cataract.
k: Rizal trained under Louis de Wecker (Paris, 1885).

q: The first optometric clinic doing refraction and the first lens-grinding shop in Manila were established in 1902 by:
w: R.M. Clark set up the first refracting clinic and lens-grinding shop in Manila in 1902, fifteen years before the profession was regulated.
k: R.M. Clark, 1902: first refracting clinic.

q: The first university-affiliated optometry college in the Philippines was:
w: CEU opened the first university-affiliated optometry college in 1928, with Dr. Roy A. Thorson as first dean. The Philippine College of Optometry was the first school overall.
k: CEU 1928 = first university-affiliated college.

q: Registration Certificate No. 1 was issued in 1918 to:
w: PRC records show Certificate No. 1 issued 11 March 1918 to Dr. Manuel Sabater. Federico Sarabia Sr. is often cited as the reputed first, being the first Filipino to study optometry in the US.
k: Certificate No. 1 = Manuel Sabater (1918).

q: The 1979 merger of OAP, PODA and PROA produced:
w: The PRC wanted a single association, so OAP, PODA and PROA merged into SOP in 1979, with about 37 OAP chapters joining.
k: 1979: OAP + PODA + PROA = SOP.

q: IFAPAO was founded at the 1st Asian-Pacific Optometric Congress held in 1978 in:
w: The 1st APOC in Manila, July 1978, founded IFAPAO, which later became APCO. Its first president, elected at the 2nd APOC in Bali, was Claro M. Cinco.
k: IFAPAO founded 1978, 1st APOC Manila.

q: The first International Optometrist of the Year, in 1988, was:
w: Dr. Claro M. Cinco received the first award in 1988. Later recipients include Svein Hommerstad and Henry W. Hofstetter, of the Hofstetter amplitude formulas.
k: First International Optometrist of the Year: Claro M. Cinco (1988).

q: Under the Code of Ethics, using the title 'Doctor' is unethical unless it is:
w: The Code requires 'Dr.' to be qualified so the public is not misled into thinking the practitioner is a physician.
k: 'Dr.' must be qualified with optometry/optometrist.

q: Displaying a name or title at street level is unethical when the letters exceed:
w: Letters larger than six inches at street level are treated as advertising that degrades the professional image, as are signboards with pictures of eyes or eyeglasses.
k: Street-level lettering limit = six inches.

q: A consultant who receives a referred patient must:
o: Report the referrer to the Board
Return the patient to the referrer
w: The Code requires the consultant to return the patient. Treating a colleague's patient in an emergency is allowed, but the patient is returned afterward.
k: Consultant returns the patient to the referrer.

q: Under the Code, a patient's character flaws learned during care are:
o: Held in trust, disclosed only by law
w: The AOA supplement holds such knowledge in trust. Confidentiality yields only to a legal requirement.
k: Patient's character flaws held in trust.

q: Which act is expressly unethical under the Code?
o: Charging by skill and time spent
w: The list of unethical acts includes lay examiners, soliciting patients, house-to-house canvassing, mobile canvassing units, advertising fees, and practicing under a name other than the one on the certificate.
k: Lay persons must never do the visual examination.

q: Under the Code, an optometrist discovering a corrupt or dishonest colleague should:
w: The duty toward other optometrists includes exposing corrupt or dishonest members to the Board, alongside cooperation and avoiding derogatory hints about colleagues.
k: Duty to expose dishonest colleagues to the Board.

q: May an optometrist refuse to accept a patient, even as the only practitioner in town?
w: The privilege to practice does not compel him to accept anyone; nothing in the Code forces service even in an emergency. Once he accepts a case needing continuing care, though, he must see it through.
k: Right to choose patients is inherent in the privilege.

q: An optometrist's instruments and library are:
o: Exempt from execution as tools of the trade
w: Exemption of instruments and library from execution is a right incidental to the privilege to practice, protecting the tools of the trade from creditors.
k: Instruments and library exempt from execution.

q: The classic statement of a patient's right to self-determination comes from:
w: Justice Cardozo wrote in Schloendorff (1914) that every adult of sound mind has the right to determine what shall be done with his own body. Natanson v. Kline and Salgo v. Leland Stanford developed it further.
k: Schloendorff (Cardozo, 1914) = right to self-determination.

q: The optometrist-patient relationship is described as fiduciary, meaning it is built on:
w: A fiduciary relationship obliges the optometrist to disclose the true facts; misrepresentation makes him liable. The relationship is also contractual, so a procedure outside that contract is void.
k: Fiduciary = built on trust, duty to disclose.

q: Consent is NOT required when the law itself compels a procedure, such as:
w: Compulsory immunization under RA 3573 and PD 996 is compelled by law under the state's police power, and refusal is punishable. The other exception is a genuine emergency.
k: Compulsory immunization (RA 3573, PD 996) needs no consent.

q: A competent patient who refuses sight-saving care:
o: Loses the right to any care
May refuse; he is master of his own body
May be reported to the Board
w: The right to refuse holds even when treatment could save life or sight. The state can still compel narrow public-health measures such as immunization under police power.
k: Competent patient may refuse even sight-saving care.

q: When an optometrist withdraws from a case, he must:
w: The relationship ends on recovery, patient withdrawal or optometrist withdrawal. On withdrawing he passes records to the successor and informs the patient. The patient may discharge him at any time.
k: On withdrawal: hand over records, inform the patient.

q: The PRC is composed of one Chairperson and how many Commissioners?
w: One full-time Chairperson and two full-time Commissioners, appointed by the President for a seven-year term with no reappointment.
k: PRC = 1 Chair + 2 Commissioners.

q: The term of a PRC Chairperson or Commissioner is:
w: The PRC term is seven years with no reappointment. Do not swap this with the Board of Optometry's five-year non-renewable term: it is a classic trap.
k: PRC term 7 years; Board term 5 years.

q: The minimum age for a PRC Chairperson or Commissioner is:
w: A PRC Chairperson or Commissioner must be at least 40, hold a valid license, and have at least five years of executive or management experience. The Board of Optometry minimum is 35.
k: PRC official: 40 years old, 5 years management experience.

q: The PRC was created by which decree?
w: PD 223, as amended by PD 657, created the PRC and moved supervision of the boards from the Bureau of Civil Service. RA 8981, the PRC Modernization Act of 2000, later reorganized it and repealed PD 223.
k: PRC created by PD 223; modernized by RA 8981 (2000).

q: The 'Board of Examiners in Optometry' was renamed the 'Board of Optometry' by:
w: PD 223 as amended by PD 657 renamed the body. RA 8050 governs the Board's present composition and functions but did not rename it.
k: PD 223/657 renamed it the Board of Optometry.

q: An administrative decision of the Board, once appealed to the PRC, may be elevated to the:
w: Administrative cases run Board → PRC (within 15 days) → Office of the President. Criminal and civil cases go through the courts and appeal to the Court of Appeals.
k: Administrative: Board → PRC → Office of the President.

q: Under PMA v. Dr. Cruz, the President is:
o: Not bound by the association's nominees
Bound to appoint the top nominee
w: The Court held a recommendation is only advice, so the President is not bound by the list the integrated organization submits.
k: PMA v. Cruz: nominations only advise the President.

q: Under PRC Resolution No. 114, a criminal action against a non-professional practising illegally is filed with the:
w: The Board has primary responsibility for starting the action, which is filed with the Provincial or City Fiscal, who files the information in court on a prima facie case.
k: Illegal practice: file with the Provincial/City Fiscal.

q: The practice of optometry is a privilege the state grants under its:
w: Police power is the legislature's power to pass reasonable laws for public welfare, health, morality and safety. People v. Pomar and US v. Gomez Jesus are the classic cases.
k: Practice is a privilege under police power.

q: Why can a corporation not practice optometry?
o: It is barred by the Corporation Code
It cannot meet the personal qualifications
It cannot afford the license fees
w: A corporation cannot hold good moral character, a degree or an oath, so it cannot practice directly, and it may not do indirectly what it cannot do directly by hiring optometrists to sell lenses.
k: Corporation cannot practice: no personal qualifications.

q: An optometrist employed by a non-optometrist may lawfully serve:
o: Anyone, if a license is shown
The general public in the store
w: An in-house or retainer optometrist may serve the employer's own employees, not the general public. An optometrist who aids or abets a lay corporation's practice risks suspension or revocation.
k: In-house optometrist serves employees, not the public.

q: A faith healer who prays over a patient's eyes, then picks up diagnostic instruments, is:
w: The Constitution protects the freedom to believe absolutely and the freedom to act conditionally. Prayer alone is religion; using diagnostic instruments or optical devices crosses into illegal practice.
k: Prayer is religion; instruments make it illegal practice.

q: The test for negligence, from Picart v. Smith, asks whether:
o: A specialist would have done better
A prudent person would have foreseen the harm
w: Picart v. Smith sets the reasonable-person standard: if a prudent person in the actor's place would foresee harm, the law imposes a duty to take precaution, and failing to do so is negligence.
k: Picart v. Smith: the prudent-person foreseeability test.

q: An optometrist is liable for breach of contract only if he:
o: Specifically promised a cure or result
w: Safian v. Aetna: failing to cure is not a breach when no cure was ever contracted. In a breach-of-contract suit expert testimony is not required and pain and suffering is not an element. Never guarantee results.
k: Breach of contract needs a promised result.

q: Damage caused by fault or negligence where no prior contract exists is a:
w: Article 2176 of the Civil Code: whoever by act or omission causes damage through fault or negligence must pay, and absent a prior contract that fault is a quasi-delict, or tort.
k: No contract + negligence = quasi-delict (Art. 2176).

q: Written defamation is libel; spoken defamation is:
w: Article 353 of the Revised Penal Code defines defamation as a public and malicious imputation causing dishonor. Libel is written (Art. 355), slander is oral, and the elements include malice and bad faith.
k: Libel written; slander oral.

q: If a patient dies owing fees, the claim runs first against the:
w: Article 291 of the Civil Code orders the claim: spouse, then descendant of nearest degree, then ascendant of nearest degree, then brothers and sisters. Medical care counts as 'support' under Article 290.
k: Fee claim order: spouse → descendant → ascendant → siblings.

q: A patient may lawfully refuse to pay when:
o: He has no money at present
A third party promised to pay
A contingent fee did not succeed
w: Valid grounds are: the service was expressly free, the fee was contingent on a success that did not come despite the patient's cooperation, or the treatment differed from what was agreed. A third party's promise never releases the patient.
k: Contingent fee + failure = patient may refuse to pay.

q: On a referral, the fee of the consultant is owed by:
w: The attending optometrist is not liable for the consultant's fee. The patient, being of legal age and sound mind, is primarily liable.
k: Consultant's fee is owed by the patient, not the referrer.

q: Section 29's continuing-education condition for license renewal was repealed by:
w: Section 20 of RA 8981, the PRC Modernization Act of 2000, repealed the CPD-for-renewal rule as RA 8050 first wrote it, so CPD is not a hard statutory condition of base renewal. The three-year cycle itself remains.
k: RA 8981 repealed Sec 29's CPD-for-renewal rule.

q: A candidate scores a 78% general average but gets 48% in one subject. Under RA 8050, the candidate:
w: Section 20 requires at least a 75% average AND no rating below 50% in any subject. The 48% disqualifies despite the high average.
k: Section 20 requires at least a 75% average AND no rating below 50% in any subject.

q: The Board of Optometry is composed of how many members, and from how many nominees?
w: , appointed by the President on PRC recommendation, from the accredited national organization's list.
k: , appointed by the President on PRC recommendation, from the accredited national organization's list.

q: An optometrist places an ad claiming "lower fees than any clinic in town." Under RA 8050 this is:
o: Allowed if the claim is true
w: Section 32(c) prohibits advertising conveying that one optometrist is better or charges lower fees. Truth is no defense.
k: Section 32(c) prohibits advertising conveying that one optometrist is better or charges lower fees.

q: A person convicted of unauthorized practice of optometry faces what penalty?
o: A fine only, up to P5,000
w: Section 33 sets imprisonment of 1 to 8 years, or a fine of P10,000 to P40,000, or both, at the court's discretion, plus applicable administrative penalties.
k: Section 33 sets imprisonment of 1 to 8 years, or a fine of P10,000 to P40,000, or both, at the court's…

q: An optometrist instills a dilating drop without telling the patient it will blur near vision and cause light sensitivity, and without asking permission. Which principle is violated?
w: Failing to disclose effects and obtain consent violates the patient's right to make an informed decision.
k: Failing to disclose effects and obtain consent violates the patient's right to make an informed decision.

q: Choosing not to fit extended-wear lenses in a non-compliant patient with a history of microbial keratitis primarily reflects which principle?
w: Avoiding a foreseeable harm whose risk outweighs the benefit is "do no harm."
k: Avoiding a foreseeable harm whose risk outweighs the benefit is "do no harm."

q: A competent adult refuses referral for a suspicious optic disc. The most ethical action is:
o: Override the refusal and refer anyway
Refuse to see the patient again
Report the patient to the Board
w: Autonomy allows refusal, but beneficence and non-maleficence require continued care: document, re-counsel, and keep monitoring.
k: Autonomy allows refusal, but beneficence and non-maleficence require continued care: document, re-counsel…

q: Providing a faster, lesser examination to a PhilHealth patient than to a private patient with the same complaint violates:
w: Distributive justice requires the same standard of care regardless of ability to pay or status.
k: Distributive justice requires the same standard of care regardless of ability to pay or status.

q: Under RA 8050 Section 40, which duty corresponds most directly to the principle of non-maleficence?
o: Render services with equal diligence to all
Consider the visual welfare of the public as the primary responsibility
Refer for medical attention when indicated
w: Referring when a condition exceeds your competence prevents harm, the core of non-maleficence. Visual welfare is beneficence; equal diligence is justice; confidentiality protects trust/autonomy.
k: Referring when a condition exceeds your competence prevents harm, the core of non-maleficence.

q: What is the correct order of registrations to open a sole-proprietorship optometry clinic?
w: Register the name (DTI), clear the location (Barangay), get the permit (Mayor), then register for taxes (BIR).
k: Register the name (DTI), clear the location (Barangay), get the permit (Mayor), then register for taxes (BIR).

q: An optical owner instructs the optometrist to limit each eye exam to ten minutes to serve more customers. This violates:
o: Chair time is the doctor's discretion
Nothing; the owner runs the business
w: Board Resolution No. 11 (Sec 48) makes chair time, method, and procedure the sole discretion of the optometrist, free from external influence by owners or managers.
k: Board Resolution No.

q: Which advertisement is permitted under the rules?
w: Only true, honest, dignified, objective information is allowed. Price claims, free-service offers, and self-laudatory superiority claims are prohibited.
k: Only true, honest, dignified, objective information is allowed.

q: The minimum examination room area and test distance required are:
w: (Board Resolution No. 11, Sec 47).
k: (Board Resolution No.

q: After securing clinic premises, within how many days must the optometrist notify the Board of the location?
w: , with photographs, to allow inspection and monitoring.
k: , with photographs, to allow inspection and monitoring.

q: Which term describes government body that supervises the Board of Optometry?
w: PRC: Professional Regulation Commission. Government body that supervises the Board of Optometry
k: PRC = Government body that supervises the Board of Optometry

q: Which term describes 5 members, 5-year non-renewable term, under the PRC?
w: Board of Optometry: regulatory board. 5 members, 5-year non-renewable term, under the PRC
k: Board of Optometry = 5 members, 5-year non-renewable term, under the PRC

q: Which term describes issued by PRC after the oath; displayed in the clinic?
w: Certificate of registration: proof of registration. Issued by PRC after the oath; displayed in the clinic
k: Certificate of registration = Issued by PRC after the oath; displayed in the clinic

q: Which term describes renewed every 3 years with continuing education?
w: Professional license: renewable credential. Renewed every 3 years with continuing education
k: Professional license = Renewed every 3 years with continuing education

q: Which term describes requires pharmacology certification (Sec 31) to use?
w: DPA: Diagnostic Pharmaceutical Agent. Requires pharmacology certification (Sec 31) to use
k: DPA = Requires pharmacology certification (Sec 31) to use

q: Which term describes eye-muscle and vision training within optometry's scope?
w: Orthoptics: Greek orthos = straight + optikos = sight. Eye-muscle and vision training within optometry's scope
k: Orthoptics = Eye-muscle and vision training within optometry's scope

q: Which term describes rA 8050 declares optometry a regular component of it?
w: Primary health care: first-contact care. RA 8050 declares optometry a regular component of it
k: Primary health care = RA 8050 declares optometry a regular component of it

q: Which term describes all ODs integrated; it promulgates the Code of Ethics?
w: Integration (Sec 36): one national organization. All ODs integrated; it promulgates the Code of Ethics
k: Integration (Sec 36) = All ODs integrated; it promulgates the Code of Ethics

q: Which term describes e.g. comparative/price advertising, untrained DPA use?
w: Prohibited act (Sec 32): forbidden conduct. e.g. comparative/price advertising, untrained DPA use
k: Prohibited act (Sec 32) = e.g. comparative/price advertising, untrained DPA use

q: Which term describes acting in the patient's best interest?
w: Beneficence: Latin bene = good + facere = to do. Acting in the patient's best interest
k: Beneficence = Acting in the patient's best interest

q: Which term describes "Do no harm"; benefit must outweigh harm?
w: Non-maleficence: Latin male = bad + facere. "Do no harm"; benefit must outweigh harm
k: Non-maleficence = "Do no harm"; benefit must outweigh harm

q: Which term describes equal standard of care regardless of status?
w: Justice: Latin justitia = fairness. Equal standard of care regardless of status
k: Justice = Equal standard of care regardless of status

q: Which term describes understanding diagnosis, options, risks; not just a signature?
w: Informed consent: knowing agreement. Understanding diagnosis, options, risks; not just a signature
k: Informed consent = Understanding diagnosis, options, risks; not just a signature

q: Which term describes a child's agreement alongside parental consent?
w: Assent: Latin assentire = agree. A child's agreement alongside parental consent
k: Assent = A child's agreement alongside parental consent

q: Which term describes patient records and diagnoses are privileged?
w: Confidentiality: Latin confidere = to trust. Patient records and diagnoses are privileged
k: Confidentiality = Patient records and diagnoses are privileged

q: Which term describes same standard for PhilHealth and private patients alike?
w: Distributive justice: fair allocation. Same standard for PhilHealth and private patients alike
k: Distributive justice = Same standard for PhilHealth and private patients alike

q: Which term describes originated the four-principles model of bioethics?
w: Beauchamp & Childress: framework authors. Originated the four-principles model of bioethics
k: Beauchamp & Childress = Originated the four-principles model of bioethics

q: Which term describes registers TIN and books of accounts (taxes)?
w: BIR: Bureau of Internal Revenue. Registers TIN and books of accounts (taxes)
k: BIR = Registers TIN and books of accounts (taxes)

q: Which term describes clears the clinic's location to operate?
w: Barangay clearance: local location clearance. Clears the clinic's location to operate
k: Barangay clearance = Clears the clinic's location to operate

q: Which term describes allows operation after fire/sanitation inspections?
w: Mayor's permit: business permit. Allows operation after fire/sanitation inspections
k: Mayor's permit = Allows operation after fire/sanitation inspections

q: Which term describes proof of registration; displayed in the clinic?
w: COR: Certificate of Registration. Proof of registration; displayed in the clinic
k: COR = Proof of registration; displayed in the clinic

q: Which term describes current PRC ID; displayed and kept updated?
w: PIC: Professional Identification Card. Current PRC ID; displayed and kept updated
k: PIC = Current PRC ID; displayed and kept updated

q: Which term describes certificates displayed; supports 3-year renewal?
w: CPD: Continuing Professional Development. Certificates displayed; supports 3-year renewal
k: CPD = Certificates displayed; supports 3-year renewal

q: Which term describes optometrist is responsible for delegated staff acts?
w: Respondeat superior: Latin "let the master answer". Optometrist is responsible for delegated staff acts
k: Respondeat superior = Optometrist is responsible for delegated staff acts

q: Which term describes res 11 (2016) clinic rules; Res 1 (2008) advertising?
w: Board Resolution: PRC Board issuance. Res 11 (2016) clinic rules; Res 1 (2008) advertising
k: Board Resolution = Res 11 (2016) clinic rules; Res 1 (2008) advertising

q: Which term describes what the office and advertising rules guard against?
w: Commercialization: turning care into retail. What the office and advertising rules guard against
k: Commercialization = What the office and advertising rules guard against
